replies.author: @domjervis
replies.text:
@m4c4c0 I believe you will enjoy reading this. A few years ago, Cramer said, "Pfizer is more of a stock than a bond. Bristol Myers Squibb is boring. Glaxo Smith Kline is where you want to put your money." 

Had some cash at that time, so I put my money where Cramer's mouth was, bought some of each. He was pretty much on as to Pfizer. I'll give him that one. 
GSK went down, & my ego wouldn't let me sell it until I got back to break-even. So much for THAT recommendation LOL 

Now then...BMY. Bought it years ago at $23, sold half of it for $59 a few years later, & am just "playing with the bank's money" with the rest. 
Would have liked to say to him, "Mr. Cramer, the next time you deem a stock 'boring,' please let me know. I'll donate 50% of my after-tax profit to your charitable trust LOL 

I still listen to the "Bear Stearns" rant. LOVE IT!

author: @CLIFFLIX
text: The "one" advantage the large legacy news has are global live "crews". When big news explodes anywhere on the planet, legacy news crews are there locally and live from the scene. The only thing new media can do on YouTube is key up a live CNN window into their stream and comment over what is happening. Independent news can't have hundreds of reporters at a scene with multiple camera angles. They don't have crews in all places of disaster. So, in a way, new media will require legacy media content in order to "talk over" what you are seeing live. I don't see new media news folks having expansive crews around the world any time soon.

author: @JustaGuy_Gaming
text:
PBS has been an issue for a long time imo. It mostly aired the same dozen of kids shows all day, stuff that most people never watched. Then every few months they'd run a drive to raise money airing all the shows people like but are almost never on, like Dr. Who (the old series), Red Dwarf, Red Green, Black Adder etc. The stuff that was normally on maybe once a week at like 11pm is the stuff they always fell back on to raise money.



Even stuff like Bob Ross was rarely on the air except when they wanted to raise more money. Over the years it got worse and worse, too many shows not fit for children aired on it. All the worst aspects of TV when actually getting ratings don't matter because it's government funded. I remember as a kid something like Clifford Puppy Days would be on like 4 times a day, and it would air pretty much the same handful of episodes over and over.

author: @kwilliams2239
text:
It would make perfect sense if half of the digital media sites go under.  Most are essentially one-man shows.  When the founder gets bought out, the channel's impact goes to zero and they'll go out of business.  The next crop of content creators will take over. It happened in the cable network space (CNN, FNN) and will continue, though the internet will speed the process by orders of magnitude. 

Digital media is not scalable to the international scene, either.  If they just pick up wire stories, what value add to they have? Another talking head? It'll be the wire services filtering and fabricating the new, instead of the corporate media. The digital space will become the new "corporate media", without even knowing it.

Another technology will take over and life continue. It's called "creative destruction".  It's generally a good thing, though AI might make an exception to the rule.
